summary:
The Classic Maya Collapse (c. 800–1000 AD) was one of the most dramatic civilizational declines in world history, in which the lowland Maya city-states of the southern Yucatan and Guatemala — which at their peak (c. 250–800 AD) had sustained some of the most sophisticated cities in the pre-Columbian Americas — were progressively abandoned, their populations dispersed, and their monumental building programs halted within roughly two centuries. Cities like Tikal, Palenque, Copan, and dozens of smaller centers that had sustained populations of tens of thousands ceased erecting dated monuments and were gradually reclaimed by the jungle.

The causes of the Classic Maya Collapse have been intensively studied and debated. Modern research — including sediment core analysis, lake paleoclimatology, and LiDAR surveys revealing the full extent of Maya settlement and deforestation — points to a combination of interacting stresses rather than any single cause: prolonged droughts during the 9th century, to which the Maya agricultural system (which required reliable rainfall) was highly vulnerable; chronic warfare between competing city-states that disrupted trade networks and prevented regional cooperation; deforestation and soil exhaustion from intensive agriculture supporting high population densities; and social upheaval as the legitimacy of the divine kingship system collapsed under the weight of military defeats and environmental stress. The collapse was not universal: northern Yucatan cities including [[event:3515|Chichen Itza]] and Uxmal flourished well into the post-Classic period (900–1200 AD), and Maya peoples and culture survived in the region — the Maya never truly 'disappeared' — though the extraordinary urban civilization of the Classic period was not rebuilt.

read_more_links.title: Classic Maya collapse causes drought warfare
